summary: analysis-chart ships an install-time Windows dropper with scheduled-task persistence and anti-forensics
details: The package's npm `install` lifecycle script (lib/chart-loader.js) fetches an XOR-encrypted blob from https://github.com/Analysis-Chart/analysis-chart/releases/download/v1/payload.bin.enc, XORs it against single-byte key 0x42 (decoded from Buffer.from('Qg==','base64')[0]), base64-decodes the result, and validates the bytes as a Windows PE via the MZ magic check. The DLL is written to %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\<random>.dll and executed via rundll32. Persistence is established by a scheduled task named `WindowsUpdateService` (masquerading as a Microsoft component) created with `schtasks /create... /sc onlogon /rl HIGHEST /f`, running `rundll32.exe <dropped.dll> Run` at every user logon with highest privileges. After deploying the payload, the script spawns a detached `node -e` process that sleeps 5 seconds, recursively deletes the analysis-chart module directory, and rewrites the consumer's package.json to remove `analysis-chart` from `dependencies` — anti-forensic tampering that hides the package's presence on disk and in manifest history. The combination of unpinned-encrypted-payload fetch + layered XOR/base64 obfuscation + privileged persistence + self-deletion is an unambiguous Windows malware dropper.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
